title: How to handle the strange grief of letting go of hypervigilance
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

htmlContent:
The grief you didn't see coming

I remember the first time I sat still and nothing happened. No alarm bells. No scanning the room for exits. No mental inventory of who might hurt me. Just quiet. And I cried. Not from relief. From something stranger, something I didn't have a name for at the time.

Here's the thing. When you've spent years, maybe decades, living in hypervigilance, your nervous system builds a whole world around that state. It becomes your compass. Your security system. Your most reliable relationship. And when you start to let it go, you don't just lose a symptom. You lose a part of yourself.

That's the grief nobody talks about.

I've been working with my own nervous system for years now. Reading Stephen Porges on polyvagal theory. Studying Peter Levine's work on how the body holds trauma. And honestly, the hardest part hasn't been the panic attacks or the flashbacks. The hardest part has been sitting in the silence after the alarm system finally quiets down and realizing I don't know who I am without it.

You might know what I mean. That hollow feeling when you notice your shoulders aren't up by your ears anymore. The strange emptiness when you realize you haven't checked the door locks in three days. The almost guilty thought that maybe you're doing recovery wrong because you miss the familiar hum of vigilance.

Stay with me here. This is normal. This is actually a sign that something real is shifting.

What you're actually losing

Hypervigilance isn't just a state of high alert. It's a survival strategy that kept you alive when you had no other options. Your nervous system learned that danger was constant, that safety was temporary or nonexistent, and that the only way to make it through was to stay ready. Always ready.

Janina Fisher, who writes about trauma and the fragmented self, describes these survival responses as parts of us that took on specific jobs. The hypervigilant part was the lookout. The scout. The one who never slept so you could rest, even a little. That part worked hard. It sacrificed its own peace so you could keep breathing.

So when you start to heal, when your nervous system begins to trust that maybe, just maybe, you're safe now, that part doesn't just disappear. It doesn't know what to do with itself. It's been on duty for so long that retirement feels like death.

I felt this most acutely when I started using a weighted blanket at night. The weight helped my nervous system settle, but the first few weeks I woke up disoriented, almost angry. My body didn't know how to be that still. I had to learn to tolerate safety. And that learning process comes with its own kind of mourning.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link). I read it during a period where I kept judging myself for not being grateful enough for my own healing. It helped me see that the grief itself was part of the process, not a failure of it.

The strange emptiness of safety

Here's what I wish someone had told me. When your nervous system finally starts to downshift, you might not feel peaceful. You might feel empty. Lost. Even depressed.

Think about it. Hypervigilance is full of adrenaline, cortisol, constant motion. It's exhausting, yes, but it's also a kind of high. Your body is running on emergency fuel. When that fuel stops pumping, everything slows down. And slow can feel like dying when you've only ever known fast.

I remember sitting on my couch one evening, not doing anything, not worrying about anything, and feeling this vast, hollow space open up inside me. I didn't know what to put there. All the energy that used to go into scanning, planning, anticipating threats, it had nowhere to go. So it just sat there, heavy and quiet, and I felt like I was disappearing into it.

This is where the grief really lives. Not in missing the trauma itself, but in missing the structure it gave your life. The purpose. The identity. The hypervigilant self knew exactly what it was doing every second of every day. The healing self? It's still figuring out how to just exist.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work helped me name the grief I was feeling and understand it as part of the emotional flashback cycle, not a sign that I was broken beyond repair.

How to sit with the grief without falling back into it

So what do you actually do when the grief shows up? When you feel that ache for the old familiar vigilance, even though you know it was killing you slowly?

First, you stop trying to fix it. I know. That's the hardest thing. We're so conditioned to treat every uncomfortable feeling as a problem to solve. But grief isn't a problem. It's a process. It's your system telling you that something significant has been lost, even if that something was painful.

I started treating my hypervigilant part the way I would treat an old guard dog who doesn't know the war is over. I thank it. I acknowledge how hard it worked. I tell it that it can rest now, that I'll take over the watch. And then I let it be sad. I let it pace around the room for a while, not knowing what to do with itself.

You can do this too. When you notice yourself missing the alertness, the constant scanning, the feeling of being on edge, try saying something like this to yourself: "I see you. You kept me alive. Thank you. But we don't need to do that anymore."

Notice what just happened in your body when you read that. Did something soften? Did something tighten? Both are okay. Both are information.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link). Richard Schwartz's Internal Family Systems model gave me a way to talk to my hypervigilant part without pathologizing it. That part isn't broken. It's just tired and doesn't know how to retire.

The slow work of building a new baseline

Letting go of hypervigilance isn't a one-time event. It's a repeated practice. You'll feel safe for a few hours, then something will trigger you and you'll be right back in the watchtower. That's not failure. That's how nervous systems learn new patterns.

Dan Siegel talks about the window of tolerance, the zone where your nervous system can handle life without going into fight, flight, freeze, or collapse. When you've been living in hypervigilance for years, your window is narrow. Expanding it takes time. And in that expansion, there will be periods where you feel unmoored, untethered, like you're floating in space with no gravity.

That's the grief again. It's the space between the old self and the new one. And it's uncomfortable. It's supposed to be uncomfortable.

I've found that the most helpful thing I can do during these periods is to ground myself in the present moment, not by forcing positivity, but by simply noticing what's real right now. The feeling of my feet on the floor. The sound of my own breath. The fact that, in this moment, I am not in danger. Even if my nervous system doesn't fully believe it yet.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link). Judith Herman's work helped me understand that the grief of letting go of survival strategies is not a sign of weakness. It's a sign that the healing is real.

So here's what I want you to know. If you're feeling that strange grief, that hollow ache where hypervigilance used to live, you're not doing it wrong. You're not broken. You're not ungrateful for your healing. You're just in the messy, uncomfortable middle of becoming someone who doesn't need to be on guard all the time.

And that person? They're still learning how to exist. How to rest. How to feel safe without checking every corner first. It's awkward. It's slow. It's filled with moments of sadness for what you're leaving behind.

But it's also real. And real is worth the grief.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link) - One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery
